draft.introduction: Proclus’s Platonic Theology (5th century CE) systematizes the entire Neoplatonic hierarchy by reading it out of Plato’s dialogues. Book I establishes the principle that Plato’s dialogues contain a hidden, consistent theological system culminating in the ineffable One.

translation.text: The theology of Plato was revealed to very few among the ancients, and even among these few, only Plotinus and Iamblichus and their successors brought its hidden doctrine to light. For Plato scattered his theological teaching throughout his dialogues, sometimes dialectically, sometimes symbolically, sometimes by inspiration. The whole of Plato’s thought converges upon a single aim: the knowledge of the gods.
translation.original_text: ἡ τοῦ Πλάτωνος θεολογία τοῖς πολλοῖς τῶν παλαιῶν ἄγνωστος ἦν

translator_notes.note: Proclus’s claim that Plato’s dialogues encode a systematic theology is the hermeneutic foundation of late Neoplatonism. This reading method—treating philosophical texts as containing hidden sacred doctrine—influenced medieval Islamic, Jewish, and Christian scriptural interpretation.
